data.title: Trump announces reciprocal tariffs, charging half of other nation's tariffs on US (updated)
data.description:
President Donald Trump announced that he's enacting reciprocal tariffs on Wednesday, which he has dubbed "Liberation Day."

As he had pre-announced, the U.S. will impose a 25% tariff on all foreign-made automobiles as of midnight tonight.

4:26 PM ET: The U.S. will calculate all tariffs and non-monetary barriers placed by other nations against the U.S. and charge those "approximately half" of what they charge us, he said. "How can anybody be upset?" Trump said, reasoning that the U.S. isn't charge that full amount.

"They do it to us, we do it to them," he said. 

"Our taxpayers and country have been ripped off for 50 years. That’s not going to happen anymore," he said.

He said we'll use "trillions and trillions of dollars to pay down national debt... Jobs and factories will come roaring back."

Trump contended that the tariffs will increase competition and bring down prices.

Updated at 4:24 PM ET: In a period before 1913, the U.S. relied heavily on tariffs and was much richer, Trump claimed. He seems to blame the Great Depression on stopping the previous tariff policy. (However, the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, enacted in 1930, is widely blamed for putting the U.S. deeper into the Depression.)

Developing... check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: RH misses estimates, says total demand rose 17% in Q4
Bitcoin extends gains after Trump delivers tariffs strategy
data.description:
Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) rally deepened modestly Wednesday after President Donald Trump announced that he's enacting reciprocal tariffs during his highly-anticipated "Liberation Day" speech.

The digital token advanced 2.8% to $87.6K at 4:23 p.m. ET, slightly above the $86.8K level seen just moments before Trump delivered his remarks.

data.title: WW International in talks to restructure debt, handing control to bondholders
data.description:
To tackle its $1.5B debt burden and dwindling revenue, WW International (NASDAQ:WW), known more commonly as Weight Watchers, is reportedly in talks with its creditors to swap some of its debt for equity and effectively handing over control of the company to its bondholders.

According to sources familiar with the matter cited by Bloomberg, discussions are ongoing as WW International (NASDAQ:WW) has been working with PJT Partners on a potential deal, while a group of creditors has also engaged advisors.

During the company’s most recent earnings call with analysts, CEO Tara Comonte acknowledged that the “benefit of our capital-light, high gross margin, cash-generative subscription model that underpins our business is almost fully offset by the strain and cost of our balance sheet,” which includes a “significant burden” of $100M in annual interest. This sentiment was echoed by the company’s CFO, who noted that the debt burden prohibits the company from investing in growth initiatives.

Bloomberg estimates WW International’s (NASDAQ:WW) 2028 term loan is valued at only 26 cents on the dollar.

Shares closed more than 2.5% lower on Wednesday and below $1 per share for a 39th consecutive day.

data.title: Duluth Holdings announces Stephanie Pugliese as CEO
data.description:
• Duluth Holdings (NASDAQ:DLTH) on Wednesday announced the appointment of Stephanie Pugliese as its president and chief executive officer and as a member of the board of directors, effective May 5, 2025.
  • From September 2019 to May 2020, Pugliese served as President of North America for Under Armour and as its President of the Americas from June 2020 until March 2023.
  • Pugliese joined the board of Fortune Brands Innovations in March 2023 and served on the American Eagle Outfitters board from August 2024 to April 2025.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Stocks end higher ahead of Trump's tariffs announcement; Musk said to be leaving govt role
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages finished higher Wednesday, ahead of President Donald Trump outlining a plan to impose reciprocal tariffs on imports coming into the U.S.   

The S&P 500 (SP500) closed up +0.7% at 5,670.97, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.9%. Each average had fallen more than 1% early in the session. The Dow (DJI) ended +0.6%. The blue-chips gauge had lost nearly 1% earlier.

In the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 1 basis point to 4.21%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) gained 5 basis points to 3.92% as prices fell. 

President Trump was appearing at the Rose Garden at 4 pm ET, after trading closed. Investors had been waiting for details on the Trump administration's “Liberation Day” plan to impose large-scale tariffs on trading partners. 

"Improvement in the market is based on two things: first and foremost, news has been circulating that there might be lower tariffs imposed by President Trump on some goods," Daniel Jones, investing group leader of Crude Value Insights, told Seeking Alpha.

"The second is that Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum made clear that any response to the U.S. won't necessarily be ‘tit-for-tat’. It is possible that the market is interpreting this as a softer stance on both sides of the border," Jones said. "However, given how politically driven market movements have been as of late, investors should not expect meaningful upside from this point on until the dust has settled."

Sky News reported Wednesday the administration was aiming to band levies by country and industry. 

Shares of Trump Media & Technology Group (DJT) fell -7.4% on Wednesday. Meanwhile, Tesla (TSLA) shares +5.5%, reversing course after a Politico report said Trump had told his inner circle that Elon Musk would be stepping down from his government role and returning to his businesses, citing three Trump insiders. The White House's press secretary in a post on X called the report "garbage." 

Musk has become an increasingly polarizing figure since fronting Trump's initiative to slash the federal government's size, and ire against him appears to have dented sales of his Tesla (TSLA) electric vehicles. 

Tesla's (TSLA) share-price gain helped drive the Consumer Discretionary sector (XLY) up +2%. Overall, nine of the 11 S&P 500 (SP500) sectors rose. 

Ahead of the tariff announcement, the ADP National Employment Report released showed March U.S. private sector employment came in at +155K vs. +120K consensus and +84K prior, upwardly revised from +77K. 

Elsewhere in markets, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) -0.4% at 103.88. "The harsher the [tariffs] announcement is perceived to be, the greater the likelihood that the dollar will weaken, as traders factor in a scenario of slower US growth, potentially forcing the Federal Reserve to cut rates faster than previously expected," Ricardo Evangelista, senior analyst at ActivTrades, said. 

For the Federal Reserve, a strategy of “looking through” tariffs is weaker now than during Trump 1.0 because inflation is running above target, Dario Perkins, managing director of global macro at GlobalData TS Lombard, said. 

The Fed “won’t cut interest rates until there are unambiguous signs of weakness in the economy (particularly in the labor market). And that is bad news for financial markets, particularly compared to the situation in 2024 when the Fed was cutting rates pre-emptively,” Perkins said in a note.

Wall Street's major averages finished mixed Tuesday in the jumpstart of Q2 action.

data.title: Trinity Capital provides $68.75M to CMR Surgical
data.description:
• Trinity Capital (NASDAQ:TRIN) announced on Wednesday a commitment of up to $68.75M in growth capital to CMR Surgical, a global medical devices company.  The investment forms part of the company's recently closed financing round of more than $200M.
  • Headquartered in Cambridge, United Kingdom, CMR Surgical is dedicated to collaborating with surgical teams and hospital partners to make robotic minimal access surgery universally accessible and affordable. 
  • The company was recently granted marketing authorization by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the use of Versius in Cholecystectomy.
  • This growth capital will enable CMR Surgical to continue to scale operations and accelerate the commercial expansion of their Versius Surgical Robotic System to more hospitals around the world.

data.title: GigaCloud Technology raises share buyback program by $16M
data.description:
• GigaCloud Technology (NASDAQ:GCT) has approved an additional $16M to its Class A ordinary share repurchase program, bringing the total authorization to $62M from its previously authorized $46M. The program runs through August 28, 2025.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Lumen Technologies snaps seven straight sessions of losses
data.description:
Lumen Technologies (NYSE:LUMN) shares snapped seven straight sessions of losses on Wednesday, as the stock closed 4.7% higher to $4.02.

The company lost about 20% in the preceding seven sessions. The stock has lost more than 25% in the last one year, compared to the over 4% fall in the broader S&P 500 Index.

LUMN is down 15% over the past one month. The stock closed 2% lower on Tuesday at $3.84.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, LUMN has a Hold rating with a score of 3.30 out of 5. The company received A+ for momentum, while it got a C- in the prospect of growth.

Turning to the Wall Street community, two analysts gave LUMN a Strong Buy. Nine analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and one recommended Sell.

Seeking Alpha analysts are also bullish and see the stock as a Buy.

Last week, Bloomberg News reported that U.S. telecom AT&T (T) is in discussions to buy the consumer fiber business of Lumen Technologies and the deal, which has not been finalized, could be valued at over $5.5B.

A recent Seeking Alpha analyst by The Asian Investor said if “Lumen can reduce its leverage and pay down its formidable debt, shares could be ripe for an upside revaluation.”

data.title: CRISPR Therapeutics snaps six straight sessions of losses
data.description:
CRISPR Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CRSP) shares snapped six straight sessions of losses on Wednesday, as the stock closed 3% to $34.18.

The Switzerland-based biotech company lost about 19% in the preceding six sessions. The stock has lost more than 13% in the last one year, compared to the over 4% fall in the broader S&P 500 Index.

CRSP is down 21.5% over the past one month. The stock closed 2.6% lower on Tuesday at $33.14.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, CRSP has a Hold rating with a score of 2.75 out of 5. The score was dragged down as the stock received C for momentum and valuation factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 17 analysts gave CRSP a Buy and above. 10 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and one recommended Strong Sell.

Seeking Alpha analysts are also bullish and see the stock as a Buy.

“Upcoming milestones and updates on CRISPR Therapeutics is currently sitting at historical support, at a valuation that is clinical assets are expected to drive shareholder value back to previous highs,” argued a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

The stock, in February, was upgraded by Evercore on upcoming catalysts, with the brokerage saying that it was now including the CTX320 and CTX310 in vivo programs in its price target, with probabilities of success of 10% and 5%, respectively.
